title: Thread-safe Supabase access in MemoryTools â€” wrap shared client in RLock
depends_on: T-104 (so track_silent is available for catching corrupted-state errors)
current_state: MemoryTools holds a single Supabase client (self.supabase). It is accessed from: (a) the main thread during memory_read/write/etc., (b) a daemon thread spawned by _bump_access at tools_memory.py:884-901 that calls _bump_access_sync, (c) the watcher manager daemon thread that may write watcher events, (d) the Telegram polling thread when it calls agent methods that touch memory. The Supabase Python client maintains internal HTTP session state and connection pooling that is NOT documented as thread-safe.
structural_problem: Concurrent calls from main thread and daemon threads can corrupt the client's internal state machine, surface as random HTTP errors, or in worst case mix request/response data. The grep shows ~25+ call sites in tools_memory.py â€” all share the same client without coordination.
target_state: Add self._supa_lock = threading.RLock() in MemoryTools.__init__. Every site that calls self.supabase.table(...).insert/update/delete/select.execute() is wrapped in 'with self._supa_lock:'. RLock allows re-entrant calls from the same thread (no deadlock if a method that already holds the lock calls another method that takes it).
design_notes: Use threading.RLock (re-entrant), NOT threading.Lock â€” same-thread re-acquire would deadlock with plain Lock
Wrap ONLY the Supabase call, not surrounding logic â€” keep critical sections small to reduce contention
Pattern: 'with self._supa_lock: result = self.supabase.table(...).insert(entry).execute()' â€” then use result outside the lock
For 'fetch then update' patterns, may need to hold the lock across both calls â€” case-by-case based on whether the result is used to gate the update
Helper method recommended: _supa(lambda: self.supabase.table(...)...) that wraps in lock + tracks failures via track_silent â€” reduces boilerplate
success_criteria: All self.supabase.table(...) calls in tools_memory.py are inside 'with self._supa_lock' block (verified by grep)
10-thread stress test (each thread calls memory_read 50x) completes without exceptions
No measurable latency regression on single-threaded usage (benchmark before/after, < 5% delta)
risk_notes: Lock contention if a Supabase call is slow (network latency) â€” daemon threads will queue up. Mitigation: RLock + small critical sections + acceptance that this is a correctness fix; the alternative (corrupt client state) is worse.
migration_plan: 1. Add 'self._supa_lock = threading.RLock()' in MemoryTools.__init__ (near self._sync_lock which already exists)
2. (Recommended) Add private helper '_supa_call(self, fn, *, category="memory.supabase")' that wraps fn() in the lock + try/except + track_silent
3. Walk every self.supabase.table(...) site in tools_memory.py. For simple cases use 'with self._supa_lock:'. For repeated patterns convert to _supa_call helper.
4. Pay attention to fetch-then-update patterns: tools_memory.py:912-929 (read row, conditional update). The lock must span both calls to prevent another thread mutating in between.
5. Add testing/test_supabase_thread_safety.py with a mock Supabase that records call ordering; spawn 10 threads each doing 50 mixed operations; assert no thread sees torn state.
6. Benchmark: run testing/test_memory.py (non-costly subset) before and after; record timing delta in solution entry.
test_plan: test_concurrent_inserts â€” 10 threads each insert 10 unique entries; all 100 land in mock; no exception raised
test_concurrent_read_write â€” 5 reader threads, 5 writer threads, 100 ops each; consistency check on final state
test_reentrant_calls â€” a method that holds the lock calls another method that also takes it; no deadlock
test_lock_released_on_exception â€” Supabase mock raises; lock still released; subsequent call succeeds
